examples.name: bubble_sort
examples.title: Bubble sort
examples.desc: Real sliding swaps; array + compare + swap.

examples.name: two_pointer
examples.title: Two pointer
examples.desc: lo/hi index carets scanning inward on a sorted array.

examples.name: stack_queue
examples.title: Stack queue
examples.desc: LIFO stack + FIFO queue, with action-point carets.

examples.name: linked_list
examples.title: Linked list
examples.desc: Singly / doubly / circular — classic node anatomy + pointer re-threading.

examples.name: textbook-doubly-list
examples.title: Textbook doubly list
examples.desc: The paper/support TEXTBOOK treatment on the algo kit — proof it's domain-agnostic: template("paper") + a hatched support rule turn the doubly linked list into a flat exam-paper figure (the theme's palette remap inks it automatically), then it inserts and removes.

examples.name: hashmap
examples.title: Hashmap
examples.desc: Hash a key to a bucket; collisions chain on (separate chaining).

examples.name: graph
examples.title: Graph
examples.desc: Labelled nodes + edges, drawn on; reflowing links.

examples.name: graph_moving
examples.title: Graph moving
examples.desc: Drag a vertex and its incident edges follow.

examples.name: bfs_dfs
examples.title: Bfs dfs
examples.desc: The same graph, queue vs stack, with live frontier readouts.

examples.name: dijkstra
examples.title: Dijkstra
examples.desc: Weighted edges, settling distances, a shortest-path tree.

examples.name: cubic-graphs
examples.title: Cubic graphs
examples.desc: A hand-composed graph-theory plate: five 3-connected simple cubic graphs (including the 6-spoke wheel from a for loop) drawn on in sequence, with LaTeX node labels ($u_i$, $v_i$) and typeset names $G_1..G_5$ — a paper-style figure straight from a theorem statement.

examples.name: calculus-demo
examples.title: Calculus demo
examples.desc: The flagship: two big ideas on one curve.

examples.name: reactive-integral
examples.title: Reactive integral
examples.desc: The reactive-math gold example: integration by parts stays one continuous equation, then a plot verifies that the antiderivative's slope is the integrand and +C is a family of vertical translations.

examples.name: reactive-world
examples.title: Reactive world
examples.desc: The foundation for creator-reactive stories: five named step blocks keep one curve, equation, tangent, live slope, derivative plot and caption synchronized.

examples.name: sine_wave
examples.title: Sine wave
examples.desc: axes + plot, a curve traced on, then vectors.

examples.name: function_graph
examples.title: Function graph
examples.desc: Plot an expression straight from a formula string.

examples.name: area_under_curve
examples.title: Area under curve
examples.desc: Riemann rectangles sweeping to the integral.

examples.name: riemann_rainbow
examples.title: Riemann rainbow
examples.desc: Coloured Riemann rectangles revealed one by one.

examples.name: riemann_readout
examples.title: Riemann readout
examples.desc: Running sums shown as a live computed number.

examples.name: tangent
examples.title: Tangent
examples.desc: The tangent line to a curve, sliding along it — its tilt is read from the function itself, so it's always the true slope (flat at the peaks).

examples.name: analysis
examples.title: Analysis
examples.desc: Ask one curve everything at once — tangent, a live slope number, the normal, the area sweeping open beneath it, and the integral climbing to its true value.

examples.name: newton
examples.title: Newton
examples.desc: Newton's method, drawn as a zig-zag: from a first guess, slide down each tangent to the axis, back up to the curve, and watch the guesses walk to the root.

examples.name: inverse-derivatives
examples.title: Inverse derivatives
examples.desc: Why a function and its inverse have reciprocal slopes: e^x and ln x mirrored across y = x, with the slopes at matching points multiplying to 1.

examples.name: derivative-of-ln-x
examples.title: Derivative of ln x
examples.desc: A visual proof of (ln x)' = 1/x: one continuous 3D coordinate plane turns over, x/y exchange screen roles, and unchanged algebra terms retain their identity.

examples.name: spline
examples.title: Spline
examples.desc: Interpolation: one smooth curve drawn through a scattered set of points — it passes through every knot exactly.

examples.name: trajectory
examples.title: Trajectory
examples.desc: A phase portrait: three paths flowing under a differential system, each spiralling into the sink at the origin.

examples.name: band
examples.title: Band
examples.desc: The area trapped between two curves, filled directly with band(top,bottom) while both boundary plots remain visible.

examples.name: curve-features
examples.title: Curve features
examples.desc: Read a cubic by its geometry: maxima/minima where the slope is zero and an inflection where the curve changes its bend (extrema, inflections).

examples.name: ftc
examples.title: Ftc
examples.desc: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: accumulate the area under a curve, differentiate that area function, and watch the original function return.

examples.name: limit
examples.title: Limit
examples.desc: A removable discontinuity visualized as an approaching point, open circle and live finite limit at x→0.

examples.name: limit-infinity
examples.title: Limit infinity
examples.desc: A rational function settling onto its horizontal asymptote, with limit(...,inf) detecting and marking the value at infinity.

examples.name: taylor
examples.title: Taylor
examples.desc: Taylor polynomials of increasing degree closing in on sin(x), one additional approximation at a time.

examples.name: linear-algebra
examples.title: Linear algebra
examples.desc: A guided lesson, not a feature demo: five chapters that build linear algebra as one connected story.

examples.name: linear-map
examples.title: Linear map
examples.desc: What a 2×2 matrix does to space: the grid deforms and the basis lands on its columns (linmap), the unit square's area becomes the determinant (determinant), and two directions only stretch — the eigenvectors (eigen).

examples.name: linear-system
examples.title: Linear system
examples.desc: The geometry of solving and spanning, in three panels: a 2×2 system as two lines crossing at the solution (linsolve), two independent vectors reaching the whole plane, and two parallel vectors collapsing to a line — rank 1 (span).

examples.name: diagonalise
examples.title: Diagonalise
examples.desc: A = P D P⁻¹ made visual: every real-diagonalisable matrix has a basis — its eigenvectors — in which it does nothing but *stretch* each axis.

examples.name: rref
examples.title: Rref
examples.desc: Gaussian elimination, animated: an augmented matrix [A | b] is reduced to reduced row-echelon form one row operation at a time, the numbers transforming in place until the left block is the identity and the last column is the solution (rref).

examples.name: projection
examples.title: Projection
examples.desc: One idea, two faces: orthogonal **projection** drops a vector onto a subspace (the shadow is the closest point, the error meets the space at a right angle), and **least-squares** fits a line to data the same way — minimising the squared residuals (project, leastsquares).

examples.name: matrix
examples.title: Matrix
examples.desc: A bracketed matrix, rows/columns addressable via tags.

examples.name: matrix_addition
examples.title: Matrix addition
examples.desc: Two matrices summed, cell by cell.

examples.name: matrix_addition_plane
examples.title: Matrix addition plane
examples.desc: The same sum, laid out on a coordinate plane.

examples.name: linear_transform
examples.title: Linear transform
examples.desc: A 2x2 matrix shearing a grid + basis vectors.

examples.name: table
examples.title: Table
examples.desc: A ruled table; cells, rows, columns, labels all addressable.

examples.name: table_braces
examples.title: Table braces
examples.desc: A table annotated with braces.

examples.name: statistics
examples.title: Statistics
examples.desc: A guided lesson, not a feature demo: describe a dataset, meet the normal curve, then see *why* the bell is everywhere (the Central Limit Theorem).

examples.name: histogram
examples.title: Histogram
examples.desc: The shape of a dataset: a list of numbers binned into bars that stagger in one at a time, with the mean marked and the range labelled (histogram).

examples.name: summary
examples.title: Summary
examples.desc: Describe a dataset in one call: the numbers as dots on a number line, with the mean, median and mode marked, a ±1σ spread band, and readouts of the range, variance and standard deviation (summary).

examples.name: boxplot
examples.title: Boxplot
examples.desc: The five-number summary as a box-and-whisker: the box spans Q1→Q3 (its width is the interquartile range), a line marks the median, the whiskers reach the rest, and a value far outside is flagged as an outlier (boxplot).

examples.name: skew
examples.title: Skew
examples.desc: Which way does the tail point? A histogram with the mean and median marked and a labelled skewness — when the mean is dragged right of the median, the data is right-skewed (skew).

examples.name: bellcurve
examples.title: Bellcurve
examples.desc: The normal (Gaussian) bell curve and the 68-95-99.7 rule: the bell draws in, then the ±1σ / ±2σ / ±3σ bands shade one at a time, showing that 68% of values fall within one standard deviation, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three (bellcurve, alias gaussian).

examples.name: clt
examples.title: Clt
examples.desc: The Central Limit Theorem — the flagship: however flat a single die is, the *average* of five dice, taken 1200 times, piles into a bell that hugs the normal curve (clt).

examples.name: correlation
examples.title: Correlation
examples.desc: Do two things move together? The scatter of paired data, the best-fit line, and the Pearson correlation r — near +1 a tight upward line, near −1 downward, near 0 a shapeless blob (correlation).

examples.name: lln
examples.title: Lln
examples.desc: The Law of Large Numbers: flip a fair coin over and over and track the running proportion of heads.

examples.name: hypothesis
examples.title: Hypothesis
examples.desc: Is a result surprising enough to be real? Under the null hypothesis the test statistic follows the standard normal; the observed z cuts off tails whose area is the p-value.

examples.name: covariance
examples.title: Covariance
examples.desc: Covariance as signed area: a cross at the means, and a rectangle from each point to the centre — cyan where x and y agree, magenta where they disagree.

examples.name: bayes
examples.title: Bayes
examples.desc: Bayesian updating: a prior belief about a coin's bias, the likelihood from the data, and the posterior that combines them — pulled toward the evidence and sharpening as it accumulates (bayes).

examples.name: probability
examples.title: Probability
examples.desc: A probability & sampling playground in four chapters: named distributions (uniform / exponential / binomial / Poisson), a confidence interval, a Monte-Carlo estimate of π, and a random walk (distribution, confidence, montecarlo, randomwalk).

examples.name: pendulum
examples.title: Pendulum
examples.desc: One pendulum shown four ways from a single deterministic swing: the motion (with a velocity arrow + KE/PE bars), the phase portrait (θ vs ω), a time series, the potential-energy well, and energy over time (pendulum + phase/timegraph/ well/energygraph + swing).

examples.name: timing-v2-scene
examples.title: Timing v2 scene
examples.desc: Generic Timing v2 controlling an ordinary physics scene: one named-phase clock schedules the intro, pendulum motion and finish independently from its native timer look.

examples.name: zeroth-law-thermodynamics
examples.title: Zeroth law thermodynamics
examples.desc: The Zeroth Law told through three particle-filled bodies: thermal relations connect, the bodies settle onto one temperature axis, and equilibrium becomes visible.

examples.name: pendulum-damped
examples.title: Pendulum damped
examples.desc: The same four views with friction on (damping): the swing decays, the phase loop spirals inward, the well ball settles, and the total-energy line drops — dissipation told the same way by every panel.

examples.name: pendulum-annotated
examples.title: Pendulum annotated
examples.desc: A guided anatomy lesson proving physics composes with base manic: section chapters, text / arrow / bracelabel annotations, and show/recolor/flash/pulse all driving the sim's parts — no special physics mode.

examples.name: spring
examples.title: Spring
examples.desc: A mass on a spring (simple harmonic motion) drawn with a real stretching coil — the same generic views on a *different* system; note the energy well is a **parabola** (½kx²) rather than the pendulum's cosine (spring + the views + run).

examples.name: spring-damped
examples.title: Spring damped
examples.desc: The damped spring: the coil's oscillation decays, the phase ellipse spirals in, the ball settles in the parabola, and total energy bleeds away.

examples.name: spring-annotated
examples.title: Spring annotated
examples.desc: Elevating the spring with a TYPEWRITER lab-note (type + cursor) and LIVE COUNTERS (counter + to(_, value, …)) ticking k and the period up — Hooke's law → parabolic well → SHM, with no stage-covering section cards.

examples.name: spring-paper
examples.title: Spring paper
examples.desc: The SAME spring sim dressed as a textbook figure AND run: template("paper") inks it, a hatched support wall, a forest-green coil and outlined mass box, Hooke's law and x₀ revealed, then run plays the SHM — the paper treatment on a LIVE sim (see pulley-paper).

examples.name: double-pendulum
examples.title: Double pendulum
examples.desc: Deterministic chaos: two arms hinged end-to-end whose outer bob traces a wild, unrepeatable curve — yet the render is frame-identical every run.

examples.name: spring-pendulum
examples.title: Spring pendulum
examples.desc: An elastic pendulum — a bob on a springy rod (drawn as a stretching coil) that both swings and bounces, energy sloshing between the two modes (springpendulum).

examples.name: kapitza
examples.title: Kapitza
examples.desc: The Kapitza pendulum: vibrate the pivot fast enough and the **inverted** position becomes stable — the bob hovers near the top instead of falling (kapitza).

examples.name: cart-pendulum
examples.title: Cart pendulum
examples.desc: A pendulum on a spring-mounted cart rolling on a track — the classic control-theory system; cart and bob trade momentum and energy (cartpendulum).

examples.name: compare-pendulum
examples.title: Compare pendulum
examples.desc: Sensitive dependence: two identical driven pendulums started 0.001 rad apart drift onto completely different paths — the butterfly effect, watched in phase/timegraph (comparependulum).

examples.name: vertical-spring
examples.title: Vertical spring
examples.desc: A mass bobbing on a vertical spring under gravity — gravity shifts the equilibrium but the energy well stays a parabola (verticalspring).

examples.name: spring-incline
examples.title: Spring incline
examples.desc: A mass on a spring on an inclined plane; gravity's along-ramp component sets a new stretched rest point it oscillates about (springincline).

examples.name: bungee
examples.title: Bungee
examples.desc: A bungee jump: free-fall, then a ONE-SIDED elastic cord (it only pulls) catches and bounces the jumper — note the lopsided energy well (bungee).

examples.name: resonance
examples.title: Resonance
examples.desc: A driven spring pushed near its natural frequency √(k/m): the amplitude climbs and climbs — resonance, watched building up in phase/energygraph (resonance).

examples.name: double-spring
examples.title: Double spring
examples.desc: Two masses coupled by springs between walls — push one and the energy sloshes back and forth (beating); normal modes show as diagonals in phase (doublespring).

examples.name: series-parallel-springs
examples.title: Series parallel springs
examples.desc: The same mass on springs in series (soft, slow) vs parallel (stiff, fast), side by side — the timegraph makes the frequency difference obvious (seriesparallel).

examples.name: car-suspension
examples.title: Car suspension
examples.desc: A quarter-car riding a scrolling road — a speed bump, a washboard stretch, and a pothole — its spring+damper soaking up the ride (carsuspension).

examples.name: car-suspension-annotated
examples.title: Car suspension annotated
examples.desc: A marketing hero: the quarter-car suspension on a template("paper") brochure page, elevated with generic base-manic — a live counter (sprung mass), leader-arrow callouts, and an energygraph of the shock being absorbed — riding a scrolling road.

examples.name: piston
examples.title: Piston
examples.desc: An engine piston: a spinning crank + connecting rod turn rotation into the piston's up-and-down stroke — the slider-crank mechanism (piston).

examples.name: molecule
examples.title: Molecule
examples.desc: A molecule as balls and springs — atoms bonded on every side, vibrating about their equilibrium shape with the total energy conserved (molecule).

examples.name: robot-arm
examples.title: Robot arm
examples.desc: A two-link robot arm reaching for a target: the joint rates come from the analytic inverse Jacobian, so the arm drives its end-effector to the goal and settles there — inverse kinematics as a solved motion (robotarm).

examples.name: pulley
examples.title: Pulley
examples.desc: The Atwood machine: two masses over one pulley, the heavier one accelerating down at (m₁−m₂)g/(m₁+m₂).

examples.name: pulley-scale
examples.title: Pulley scale
examples.desc: The surprise every physics class remembers: an in-line spring scale on an Atwood machine reads the rope TENSION 2·m₁·m₂·g/(m₁+m₂) — not the sum of the two weights (pulleyscale).

examples.name: block-tackle
examples.title: Block tackle
examples.desc: A compound pulley (block & tackle): a load on a movable block held by N rope strands, pulled by an effort mass.

examples.name: compound-pulley
examples.title: Compound pulley
examples.desc: A compound pulley with a MOVABLE pulley: a fixed top pulley carries mass A on one side and a movable lower pulley on the other; that pulley carries B and C.

examples.name: incline-pulley
examples.title: Incline pulley
examples.desc: The incline-Atwood: a block on an incline tied over a pulley at the top to a hanging mass.

examples.name: double-incline
examples.title: Double incline
examples.desc: Two blocks on a wedge's two slopes, tied over a pulley at the apex (right slope rough).

examples.name: incline-bumper
examples.title: Incline bumper
examples.desc: A block slides down an incline into a spring bumper at the base, compresses it, and launches back up — one-sided contact, gravity PE ↔ kinetic ↔ spring PE, energy conserved (inclinebumper).

examples.name: collide-blocks
examples.title: Collide blocks
examples.desc: The classic momentum demo: block 1 hangs on a spring to the wall, block 2 slides in and they collide.

examples.name: collide-blocks-annotated
examples.title: Collide blocks annotated
examples.desc: Conservation of momentum, the MANIC way — not a 1:1 port of the lab sim but a guided lesson: the live Σp readout as the star, the KE↔spring-PE energy view, staged callouts, and honest narration (with a wall-spring, Σp is conserved AT each collision, not constant).

examples.name: bullet-block
examples.title: Bullet block
examples.desc: A bullet fired into a block EMBEDS (perfectly inelastic).

examples.name: bullet-impact
examples.title: Bullet impact
examples.desc: BEST OF BOTH: the cinematic gun-shot (gun · muzzle flash · a flying cam/zoom · BOOM) wrapped around the REAL bulletblock physics — the collision is genuinely inelastic, the live speed readout actually collapses 40 → ~1, and the BOOM is synced to the true impact.

examples.name: bullet-block-annotated
examples.title: Bullet block annotated
examples.desc: The bullet's JOURNEY, the manic way: a gun fires, a muzzle flash, a glowing bullet crosses the gap in slow-motion and embeds — the speed readout crashing 40 → ~1.

examples.name: newtons-cradle
examples.title: Newtons cradle
examples.desc: Newton's cradle: pull one ball, one swings out the far side — momentum and energy pass straight through the chain.

examples.name: string-wave
examples.title: String wave
examples.desc: A wave on a plucked string: 36 masses on springs, both ends fixed (the discretised wave equation).

examples.name: loop-track
examples.title: Loop track
examples.desc: A ball rolls down a ramp and around a vertical LOOP-THE-LOOP — the curved-track case.

examples.name: loop-cinematic
examples.title: Loop cinematic
examples.desc: The loop-the-loop as a MOVIE with real physics inside: the camera pushes in as the ball climbs, and the tension is genuine — a modest release height means it truly crawls over the top before rocketing out.

examples.name: spring-chain
examples.title: Spring chain
examples.desc: Three blocks joined by two springs on an incline — coupled oscillators.

examples.name: incline-showcase
examples.title: Incline showcase
examples.desc: One paper page, FOUR live incline problems: a friction ramp, an incline+pulley, a two-slope wedge, and a spring bumper — revealed one at a time with narration, then all run in parallel.

examples.name: textbook-incline-fbd
examples.title: Textbook incline fbd
examples.desc: A block on an incline as a physics-class FREE-BODY DIAGRAM: the reusable forces(id) view draws gravity/normal/friction/a vectors on the block, a second panel redraws them from a point, and template("paper") inks it — then run slides the block (ramp).

examples.name: pulley-annotated
examples.title: Pulley annotated
examples.desc: The Atwood machine elevated with CAMERA work: cam + zoom push in on the two masses for the imbalance beat and glow the heavier one, a counter ticks the acceleration up, then it pulls back to release — cinematography instead of section cards.

examples.name: pulley-paper
examples.title: Pulley paper
examples.desc: The SAME Atwood sim dressed as a textbook figure AND run: template("paper") inks it automatically, a hatched support ceiling, a forest-green wheel and outlined mass boxes, a base-manic reveal, then run plays the motion — the paper treatment on a LIVE sim.

examples.name: ramp
examples.title: Ramp
examples.desc: A block sliding down an inclined plane with static/kinetic friction — the full force model.

examples.name: drop-mass
examples.title: Drop mass
examples.desc: A mass dropped onto a spring-block STICKS — a perfectly inelastic collision.

examples.name: raft-cm
examples.title: Raft cm
examples.desc: A person walks back and forth on a floating raft; with no external force the centre of mass stays fixed, so the raft glides the opposite way — momentum conservation you can see (raft).

examples.name: brachistochrone
examples.title: Brachistochrone
examples.desc: Four beads race under gravity from A to B down a straight line, a circular arc, a parabola, and a cycloid.

examples.name: brachistochrone-annotated
examples.title: Brachistochrone annotated
examples.desc: The elevation recipe on a RACE, told with KINETIC TYPOGRAPHY: wordpop pops the question in, karaoke sweeps a highlight across the four path names as the curves sketch on, then flash/glow crown the cycloid — a third, distinct elevation style.

examples.name: textbook-pulley
examples.title: Textbook pulley
examples.desc: A physics-TEXTBOOK figure, manic style: the template("paper") white page, a hatched support ceiling, a green pulley wheel, and outlined labelled mass boxes — the classic m over 2m+3m arrangement, all base primitives.

examples.name: textbook-tension
examples.title: Textbook tension
examples.desc: Another textbook figure: two support ropes at 60°/30° meeting a knot, a string over a hanging pulley carrying 10 kg with the other end anchored to a hatched floor — support + template("paper") for the flat exam-paper look.

examples.name: refraction
examples.title: Refraction
examples.desc: Snell's law you can watch: a ray crossing from air into glass bends toward the normal, and run sweeps the incidence angle so the refracted ray swings — the live in/out read-outs are the true angles.

examples.name: lens
examples.title: Lens
examples.desc: A converging lens: a parallel beam bends to meet at the focal point F (ideal thin lens — every ray passes through F).

examples.name: prism
examples.title: Prism
examples.desc: White light into a prism, out as a RAINBOW — each colour traced through both faces with its own refractive index (real Sellmeier dispersion), so blue bends more than red because glass genuinely slows blue more.

examples.name: achromat
examples.title: Achromat
examples.desc: The optics capstone — chromatic aberration and its fix: a single lens focuses blue nearer than red, so white light never comes to one point; run sweeps in the achromatic doublet and the colours snap back to a single sharp focus (achromat).

examples.name: refraction-paper
examples.title: Refraction paper
examples.desc: Snell's law as a template("paper") TEXTBOOK figure: inked media labels, the normal, and the law itself, with a camera easing in on the bending point as run sweeps the angle — the geometric builtins suit paper (refract + annotation + camera).

examples.name: lens-paper
examples.title: Lens paper
examples.desc: The converging lens inked on paper and narrated by a TYPEWRITER caption (a different elevation lens): labelled parallel rays and focal point F while run slides the focus (lens + type).

examples.name: prism-cinematic
examples.title: Prism cinematic
examples.desc: The prism on a dark optics bench where the spectrum GLOWS (a rainbow washes out on paper): the colour names pop in word-by-word (wordpop) as the fan spreads and the camera flies toward it — KINETIC-TYPE elevation (prism).

examples.name: achromat-cinematic
examples.title: Achromat cinematic
examples.desc: The achromat with the CAMERA magnifying the focal region so the red/blue split is dramatic, a bracelabel marking the aberration gap that closes as run sweeps in the doublet and the colours merge (achromat + camera + brace).

examples.name: lens-system
examples.title: Lens system
examples.desc: A REAL multi-element lens, ray-traced through its actual spherical surfaces (not the ideal thin lens): the fast singlet reveals SPHERICAL ABERRATION — draw sketches the rays, then run sweeps a sensor plane and the live spot read-out dips but never reaches a point, because the outer rays focus short (lenssystem, presets singlet/doublet/triplet).

examples.name: ray-fan
examples.title: Ray fan
examples.desc: Reading an aberration: rayfan plots each ray's error at focus against where it entered the lens.

examples.name: spot-diagram
examples.title: Spot diagram
examples.desc: Lens quality as a picture: spotdiagram plots where a ray bundle actually lands at focus.

examples.name: lens-prescription
examples.title: Lens prescription
examples.desc: Type your OWN lens: lenssystem takes a design by name ("plano-convex", "doublet", …) OR a custom PRESCRIPTION — the designer's surface table "radius thickness glass [conic] [aperture] | …" — traced through the true surfaces with real Sellmeier glass (lenssystem).

examples.name: aspheric-lens
examples.title: Aspheric lens
examples.desc: How an ASPHERE kills spherical aberration: a spherical surface can't focus a wide beam to a point (a blur, RMS 1.5 px), but reshaping it to the right conic — one constant in the prescription — collapses every ray to a point (RMS 0.1 px).

examples.name: off-axis
examples.title: Off axis
examples.desc: The hard test — light 8° OFF the axis.

examples.name: vector_field
examples.title: Vector field
examples.desc: A magnitude-coloured vector field.

examples.name: coordinates
examples.title: Coordinates
examples.desc: Axes, planes, number lines, polar & complex planes.

examples.name: pie
examples.title: Pie
examples.desc: A pie chart built from sectors.

examples.name: equilateral
examples.title: Equilateral
examples.desc: Euclid I.1 — an equilateral triangle from two circles.

examples.name: triangle
examples.title: Triangle
examples.desc: A triangle with its centres and cevians.

examples.name: incircle_tangents
examples.title: Incircle tangents
examples.desc: The incircle and its tangent points.

examples.name: tangents
examples.title: Tangents
examples.desc: Tangent lines from a point to a circle.

examples.name: orthocenter
examples.title: Orthocenter
examples.desc: The orthocentre from the three altitudes.

examples.name: euler_line
examples.title: Euler line
examples.desc: The Euler line through centroid, circumcentre, orthocentre.

examples.name: nine_point
examples.title: Nine point
examples.desc: The nine-point circle.

examples.name: conics
examples.title: Conics
examples.desc: Ellipse, parabola, hyperbola.

examples.name: reactive-math-journey
examples.title: Reactive math journey
examples.desc: A playful vertical journey from 1+1 in Class 1 through fractions, algebra, geometry, calculus, linear algebra, probability and Fourier analysis to a PhD-level functional integral — then back to the curiosity that started it all.

examples.name: reactive-math-notation
examples.title: Reactive math notation
examples.desc: One Reels-ready stage exercises structured LaTeX across thirteen notation worlds: algebra, calculus, limits, trigonometry, logic, sums/products, physics, chemistry, biology, probability, matrices/vectors, mixed prose/math, and creator notation.

examples.name: quadratic-formula-continuity
examples.title: Quadratic formula continuity
examples.desc: The quadratic formula by completing the square with one persistent LaTeX equation.

examples.name: transforms
examples.title: Transforms
examples.desc: Apply a 2x2 matrix (ApplyMatrix) to a group.

examples.name: transform_copy
examples.title: Transform copy
examples.desc: Duplicate an entity, then transform the copy.

examples.name: morph
examples.title: Morph
examples.desc: A sampled-point shape morph from A to B.

examples.name: typewriter
examples.title: Typewriter
examples.desc: Text revealed character by character.

examples.name: captions
examples.title: Captions
examples.desc: Karaoke / word-pop caption modes.

examples.name: terminal_boot
examples.title: Terminal boot
examples.desc: The neon terminal template booting up.

examples.name: brace
examples.title: Brace
examples.desc: The curly-brace family.

examples.name: banner
examples.title: Banner
examples.desc: The manic logo / banner reveal.

examples.name: equation
examples.title: Equation
examples.desc: Display-quality LaTeX for fractions, roots, sums, powers and integrals, with semantic colour and template tinting.

examples.name: inline-math
examples.title: Inline math
examples.desc: Inline LaTeX mixed with ordinary prose, including wrapped explanatory text and a standalone display equation.

examples.name: image
examples.title: Image
examples.desc: Embed a raster image (PNG/JPG) with image(id, (x,y), "path", w, h) — a real file drawn into the scene and animated like any entity (shown, spun, pulsed, moved).

examples.name: gun-shot
examples.title: Gun shot
examples.desc: A pure-imagination SCENE — no physics kit, just storytelling: a gun fires, the camera flies along with the bullet (cam/zoom), a block drops in out of nowhere, and BOOM — flash/shake/pulse + a for-loop spark burst.

examples.name: fractal_tree
examples.title: Fractal tree
examples.desc: One recursive def, drawn to depth 12.

examples.name: particles-flow
examples.title: Particles flow
examples.desc: Contained ambient motion and live curved connections in four generic words: particles, wander, link, and flow.

examples.name: hue_wave
examples.title: Hue wave
examples.desc: An animated hue wave across a grid.

examples.name: hill_run
examples.title: Hill run
examples.desc: A little scene animated with the language layer.

examples.name: walk
examples.title: Walk
examples.desc: An articulated stick figure walking down a road — legs swing, arms counter-swing, the body bobs — built purely from the language layer (let + for + trig), no character rig.

examples.name: two_person_walk
examples.title: Two person walk
examples.desc: Two figures walk toward each other, MEET in the middle, shake hands, then continue past — a little choreographed scene from loops and arithmetic alone (the language layer as animation).

examples.name: equal_cuts
examples.title: Equal cuts
examples.desc: A circle halved again and again (pizza cuts).

examples.name: archimedes_pi
examples.title: Archimedes pi
examples.desc: Bounding pi with inscribed / circumscribed polygons.

examples.name: pieday
examples.title: Pieday
examples.desc: A Pi Day card: a rainbow petal-flower built from a loop of circles, radial rays, the digits of π, and the definition circumference / diameter = pi.

examples.name: perfect-reel
examples.title: Perfect reel
examples.desc: The gold-path production starter: phone-safe composition, real LaTeX, exact pacing, professional mono styling, creator identity, timeline markers and a focused end card.

examples.name: reactive-multiformat
examples.title: Reactive multiformat
examples.desc: One named reactive story rendered as portrait, 4:5 feed, square, or landscape with the --canvas override.

examples.name: parameter-journeys
examples.title: Parameter journeys
examples.desc: One visible parameter drives a quadratic plot, its live tangent and slope, a geometric position, scale and a derived numeric readout.

examples.name: creator-v2-options-socials
examples.title: Creator v2 options socials
examples.desc: The asset-free v2.4 review scene: collision-safe question hierarchy, professional A/B/C/D cards, uniform correct-state spacing, and native YouTube/X/web identity lockups.

examples.name: creator-v2
examples.title: Creator v2
examples.desc: The complete v2 core: responsive studio quiz, optics media, width-aware answer cards, a signature creator footer, optional explanation and a branded final end card.

examples.name: creator-v2-timing
examples.title: Creator v2 timing
examples.desc: Timing v2 in a portrait quiz: exact ask/options/think/reveal/hold phases, LaTeX media, and a segmented timer whose presentation can change without changing the choreography.

examples.name: creator-v2-timers
examples.title: Creator v2 timers
examples.desc: All six native Timing v2 looks—ring, bar, number, segments, ticks and pulse—running side by side.

examples.name: creator-v2-timing-square
examples.title: Creator v2 timing square
examples.desc: A square feed-card variant with a scaled dramatic preset and a filling tick timer, showing that timing and timer placement reflow independently across formats.

examples.name: creator-v2-olympiad-geometry
examples.title: Creator v2 olympiad geometry
examples.desc: An olympiad-level geometry Reel built as pause → predict → prove, with a responsive construction, authored explanation and reusable creator identity.

examples.name: creator-v2-latex-calculus
examples.title: Creator v2 latex calculus
examples.desc: Portrait Creator v2 with inline and display LaTeX: a calculus question, fitted formula answers and crisp typesetting throughout the timed reveal.

examples.name: creator-v2-latex-algebra
examples.title: Creator v2 latex algebra
examples.desc: Square Creator v2 on a paper surface, checking that algebraic LaTeX and answer cards remain balanced and readable outside the vertical format.

examples.name: creator-v2-latex-physics
examples.title: Creator v2 latex physics
examples.desc: Landscape Creator v2 with a physics equation, proving the same LaTeX quiz system reflows cleanly for widescreen explainers.

examples.name: quiz-skins
examples.title: Quiz skins
examples.desc: The quiz Short in a dozen lines: quiz/option/run + a creator/socials footer.

examples.name: quiz-euler
examples.title: Quiz euler
examples.desc: A quiz Short with an ANIMATED figure: the geo kit constructs the Euler line (the answer), and figure(...) AUTO-FITS the whole triangle+circumcircle into the zone between the question header and the answer cards — no coordinate tuning.

examples.name: quiz-geometry
examples.title: Quiz geometry
examples.desc: The hand-authored proof behind the kit (≈60 lines from shipped primitives): a question, an animated geometry figure, four option cards, a countdown and a time-out reveal.

examples.name: quiz-geometry-2
examples.title: Quiz geometry 2
examples.desc: A layout stress-test: a different olympiad question with TWO figures side by side (an acute triangle with its circumcentre INSIDE vs an obtuse one with it OUTSIDE), proving the 2×2 options, countdown and footer keep their spacing for richer figure content.

examples.name: boolean
examples.title: Boolean
examples.desc: Union / intersection / difference of shapes.

examples.name: three_d
examples.title: Three d
examples.desc: Cubes, spheres, arrows, a curve, a surface and solids together — the 3D basics on one stage.

examples.name: solids3
examples.title: Solids3
examples.desc: Filled, shaded solids: a prism, a cone, and a lathed vase.

examples.name: param3
examples.title: Param3
examples.desc: Parametric surfaces a height field can't make — a torus, a sphere, and a Möbius strip.

examples.name: extrude3
examples.title: Extrude3
examples.desc: Lifting flat shapes into solids, including a boolean cut-out (a plate with a hole) and an L-beam.

examples.name: morph3
examples.title: Morph3
examples.desc: Morphing across families — a cube into a sphere, a saddle into a bowl, a helix into a ring.

examples.name: linear-algebra-3d
examples.title: Linear algebra 3d
examples.desc: The 3D companion to the linear-algebra lesson: one matrix [[1,0,0],[0,3,1],[0,1,3]] (det 8; eigenvalues 1, 2, 4) seen two ways on an orbiting stage — first as a transformation (the unit cube → a parallelepiped whose volume is the determinant), then through its eigenvectors (the invariant axes that only stretch).

examples.name: linear-map3
examples.title: Linear map3
examples.desc: Linear algebra in 3D: a 3×3 matrix deforms the unit cube into a parallelepiped, with basis arrows i/j/k landing on the matrix's columns and the enclosed volume labelled as the determinant (linmap3).

examples.name: eigen3
examples.title: Eigen3
examples.desc: The real eigenvectors of a 3×3 matrix, in 3D: the invariant lines through the origin that only stretch (by λ) when the matrix acts (eigen3).

examples.name: matrix3
examples.title: Matrix3
examples.desc: A 3×3×3 block of cubes, with a shear matrix **M** and its inverse **M⁻¹** applied and undone.

examples.name: double-integral3
examples.title: Double integral3
examples.desc: Multivariable calculus: the volume under a surface as a limit of finer and finer columns — a double integral, made solid.

examples.name: multivariable3
examples.title: Multivariable3
examples.desc: Calculus on a surface: a smooth hill, its tangent plane and the gradient arrow at a chosen point, inspected with an orbiting camera.

examples.name: volume3
examples.title: Volume3
examples.desc: The volume under a surface represented as solid 3D Riemann-sum columns, turning a double integral into visible geometry.
